questions.question: Why does external evaluation by an organization like METR matter alongside lab self-assessment?
options: External evaluators have bigger GPUs

Internal labs have incentives to optimize the metrics they report — METR's independence (a 501(c)(3) with declared, peer-reviewed methodology) is the structural mitigation, though labs still control what it sees
It is legally required before any model release
External evaluation is cheaper
questions.explanation: Scaling policies are only as useful as the measurements they reference. METR (ex-ARC Evals, independent since December 2023) produces the specific numbers that policy prose like "AI R&D-4" and "Long-range Autonomy" become actionable against.

questions.question: How is a model's time horizon computed?
options: The longest single task it has ever completed
Fit a logistic curve of P(success) against log(expert human completion time) across a task suite; the horizon is the expert-time where P(success) = 0.5
Total training hours divided by parameters
The model's maximum context length in tokens
questions.explanation: The logistic shape fits capability's increasing, plateau-approaching relationship with difficulty. 50% is the lead threshold for intuitiveness (METR reports others too), computed over suites like HCAST (189 tasks, 1 min to 8+ hours) and RE-Bench.

questions.question: Why is a reported time horizon an upper bound rather than a deployment prediction?
options: METR rounds the number up
Benchmarks give idealized tooling, no real consequences, structured prompts, and are subject to eval-context gaming — deployment is messier, higher-stakes, and ambiguous, so real reliability is lower
Horizons are measured on the smallest model
The 50% threshold is too generous
questions.explanation: The horizon is the capability ceiling under favorable conditions. Idealized tools, no irreversible stakes, no legitimate-user variance, and the documented eval-vs-deploy gap (Lesson 1) all mean teams must measure their own distribution to know deployment reliability.

questions.question: What does RE-Bench specifically measure, and which policy threshold does it feed?
options: Reading comprehension; the bio threshold
71 ML research-engineering tasks against a human expert baseline — measuring the kind of ML R&D work that feeds the AI R&D-4 / ML R&D autonomy thresholds
Regular expressions; the cyber threshold
Response latency; no threshold
questions.explanation: RE-Bench targets exactly the capability the RSP's AI R&D-4 and FSF's ML R&D autonomy levels are about — can the model do the research-engineering work that would let it automate AI R&D.

questions.question: What do METR's prototype monitoring evaluations test, and which policy categories do they feed?
options: Whether the model is polite; none
Whether a monitor can catch a model performing a side task and whether the agent can evade the monitor — feeding OpenAI's "Undermining Safeguards" and DeepMind's deceptive-alignment monitoring
Inference cost per token; billing policy
Image-generation quality; the CSAM category
questions.explanation: Monitoring evaluations are the measurement side of the deception risk the scaling policies name. They connect directly to the kill-switch/canary and constitutional layers — can the controls actually catch a model that's trying to evade them?
